questions: Identify the tier of the U.S. federal judiciary that sits immediately below the Supreme Court and serves as the final arbiter for the majority of federal cases due to the high volume of rulings that are not heard by the highest court. Describe the standard composition of the decision-making panels in this tier and explain how its jurisdiction is organized regarding regional versus specialized cases. Furthermore, detail the specific evidence-based model utilized by federal supervision officers to reduce recidivism within this system, explicitly naming the three core principles of the model and the two scientific risk assessment tools used for pretrial and post-conviction stages.

logic_chain: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals
rounds.question: Identify the tier of the U.S. federal judiciary that sits immediately below the Supreme Court and serves as the final arbiter for the majority of federal cases due to the high volume of rulings that are not heard by the highest court. Describe the standard composition of the decision-making panels in this tier and explain how its jurisdiction is organized regarding regional versus specialized cases. Furthermore, detail the specific evidence-based model utilized by federal supervision officers to reduce recidivism within this system, explicitly naming the three core principles of the model and the two scientific risk assessment tools used for pretrial and post-conviction stages.
checklist_width: Panel Composition: Judges usually sit in panels of three.
Organization: 12 regional circuits (geographic) plus 1 Federal Circuit (specialized/nationwide).
Principle 1 (Risk): Focus resources on higher-risk individuals; avoid over-supervising lower-risk ones.
Principle 2 (Need): Target specific criminogenic factors (e.g., substance use, cognition).
Principle 3 (Responsivity): Tailor interventions to remove specific barriers (e.g., transportation, motivation).
checklist_depth: Target Entity 1: U.S. Courts of Appeals (identified via hierarchy position and finality of decisions).
Target Entity 2: Evidence-Based Practices/RNR Model (identified via supervision context and 'three core principles').
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep reasoning to identify the 'U.S. Courts of Appeals' based on its hierarchical position and workload statistics (Target 0) and the 'Risk-Need-Responsivity Model' based on the description of supervision practices (Target 1). It requires Wide aggregation to combine structural details of the courts (circuits, panels) with specific methodological details of probation (principles, assessment tool acronyms) found in separate sections of the source text.
source_nodes: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: About the U.S. Courts of Appeals On this page Courts of appeals review challenges to court decisions to determine whether the proceedings were fair and the law was applied cor
rounds.answer_a:
The tier immediately below the U.S. Supreme Court is the United States Courts of Appeals, also known as the Circuit Courts. They are the final arbiter for most federal cases, as the Supreme Court hears only a tiny fraction of appeals [1]. These courts primarily operate through three-judge panels randomly selected from the circuit's judges to hear and decide cases [2]. En banc reviews, involving all active judges, are rare.

Jurisdiction is organized geographically and by subject matter. There are 13 judicial circuits: 11 numbered regional circuits covering the U.S. and its territories, one for the District of Columbia, and the specialized Federal Circuit. The regional circuits hear appeals from district courts within their borders, while the Federal Circuit has nationwide jurisdiction over specific matters like patents, international trade, and veterans' claims [3].

Within this system, federal probation officers use the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model to reduce recidivism [4]. Its three core principles are: 1) **Risk:** Match supervision intensity to an offender's risk of reoffending. 2) **Need:** Target dynamic, changeable criminogenic needs. 3) **Responsivity:** Use cognitive-behavioral interventions tailored to the individual's learning style [4]. The primary evidence-based tools are the **Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA)** used in the pretrial stage and the **Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA)** used post-conviction to inform supervision and treatment plans [5].

## References
[1] United States Courts. "Court Role and Structure." www.uscourts.gov.
[2] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. "How The Courts Work - Appellate Courts." www.uscourts.gov.
[3] Federal Judicial Center. "Federal Courts and What They Do." www.fjc.gov.
[4] U.S. Courts. "Evidence-Based Practices in Corrections." www.uscourts.gov.
[5] Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. "Post Conviction Risk Assessment." www.uscourts.gov.
rounds.answer_b:
The U.S. federal judiciary tier immediately below the Supreme Court is the U.S. Courts of Appeals, the final arbiter for most federal cases due to the Supreme Court’s limited docket (hearing roughly 1% of petitions annually) [1]. Standard decision-making panels consist of 3 active circuit judges, with senior judges or visiting district judges filling vacancies; en banc reviews (full circuit judge panels) are reserved for high-stakes rulings with conflicting precedent [2]. Jurisdiction is structured into 13 regional circuits (each covering a cluster of states or territories, hearing appeals from local district courts) and 2 specialized circuits: the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit (handling patents, government contracts, and international trade appeals) and the U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims (reviewing veterans’ benefit appeal decisions) [3]. Federal supervision officers use the Evidence-Based Supervision Model (EBSM) to cut recidivism [4]. Its three core principles are the risk principle (matching supervision intensity to offender risk levels), the need principle (targeting criminogenic needs like substance abuse), and the responsivity principle (tailoring interventions to individual capabilities) [4]. Pretrial risk is assessed via the Pretrial Risk Assessment Instrument (PRAI), and post-conviction risk via the Post-Conviction Risk Assessment Instrument (PCRAI) [4].

## References
[1] U.S. Courts, "U.S. Courts of Appeals," accessed October 26, 2024, https://www.uscourts.gov/courts/courts-of-appeals.
[2] U.S. Courts, "Court Structure and Case Processing," accessed October 26, 2024, https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-structure.
[3] U.S. Courts, "Circuit Information," accessed October 26, 2024, https://www.uscourts.gov/courts/courts-of-appeals/circuit-information.
[4] U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services System, "Evidence-Based Supervision Model," accessed October 26, 2024, https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-pretrial-services/evidence-based-practices.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A provided a perfectly accurate and well-formatted response. Agent B failed on two counts: 1) Formatting (it provided a 'wall of text'), and 2) Accuracy regarding the court hierarchy. Agent B incorrectly listed the 'U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims' as being in the tier immediately below the Supreme Court; in reality, appeals from that court go to the Federal Circuit, placing it a tier lower. Agent B also got the count of regional circuits wrong (implying 13 regional, whereas there are 12 regional + 1 specialized).
